topic: Managing graphics state and isolating transformations using push() and pop() to prevent unintended side effects in a sketch.

concepts.name: push() and pop()
concepts.explanation: They act as boundaries that isolate transformations and styles. Whatever happens between a push() at the start and a pop() at the end stays contained within that block and won't 'leak' into the rest of your code.
concepts.why_it_matters: Without them, transformations like rotation or translation applied to one object will 'pollute' every subsequent object drawn in the sketch.

concepts.name: State Isolation
concepts.explanation: The idea of creating a local scope for your drawing settings so that changes to position, rotation, or color don't affect the global coordinate system.
concepts.why_it_matters: It allows you to draw complex, nested patterns without having to manually reset every single property (like stroke or fill) back to its original state.

concepts.name: Order of Operations (Translate before Rotate)
concepts.explanation: To rotate an object around its own center rather than the corner of the screen (0,0), you must translate to that object's center first, then apply the rotation.
concepts.why_it_matters: If you rotate before translating, the object will orbit around the origin (0,0) instead of spinning in place.

code_examples.demonstrates: How to isolate a rotation so it only affects the rectangle inside the push/pop block.
code_examples.common_issue: If pop() is missing, all subsequent shapes will also rotate.

code_examples.demonstrates: The correct sequence to rotate an object around its own center (Translate $\rightarrow$ Rotate).
code_examples.common_issue: Rotating before translating causes the object to swing around the screen origin (0,0).

code_examples.demonstrates: Isolating visual styles (like removing a stroke) so it doesn't affect the rest of the sketch.
code_examples.common_issue: Using noStroke() without push/pop will make every single shape drawn afterward have no outline.

mistakes_and_fixes.symptom: My shapes are rotating or moving when they shouldn't be.
mistakes_and_fixes.cause: Transformations (like rotate or translate) are 'leaking' into the rest of the sketch because they aren't isolated.
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Wrap the transformations and the drawing command in a push() and pop() block.

mistakes_and_fixes.symptom: My shape is spinning around the corner of the screen instead of its own center.
mistakes_and_fixes.cause: The rotation is being applied before the translation to the object's center.
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Use translate() to move the origin to the shape's center first, then call rotate().

mistakes_and_fixes.symptom: I turned off the stroke for one shape, and now all my other shapes have no outlines too.
mistakes_and_fixes.cause: The style change (noStroke) was applied globally and wasn't reset.
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Wrap the styling command in push() and pop().

key_moments.description: The instructor shows a 'failure' where a rectangle is unintentionally caught in a rotation loop, creating the need for push/pop.
The 'After' moment: showing how the rectangle becomes stable and independent after adding push/pop.
Explaining the necessity of Translate $\rightarrow$ Rotate to control the axis of rotation.

prerequisites: Basic use of translate() and rotate()
student_questions: Why is my shape moving when I only wanted to rotate it?
How do I stop one transformation from affecting everything else?
Why are all my shapes losing their outlines?
How do I make a shape rotate around its own center instead of the corner?
What is the difference between push and pop?
My shapes are all flying off the screen, how do I fix it?
How do I isolate a color change so it only applies to one circle?
Why does the order of translate and rotate matter?
